diagrams.title: Java Exception Hierarchy Tree
diagrams.description: A hierarchical tree diagram showing the complete exception class hierarchy in Java, from Object at the root to specific exception classes at the leaves
elements: Root: java.lang.Object
Level 1: java.lang.Throwable (base class)
Level 2: java.lang.Error (programmatic errors - JVM related)
Level 2: java.lang.Exception (application exceptions)
Level 3: java.lang.RuntimeException (unchecked exceptions)
Level 3: Other Exception subclasses (checked exceptions)
Leaf classes: NullPointerException, ArithmeticException, IOException, SQLException, ClassNotFoundException, etc.
Arrows connecting parent to child classes with labels showing inheritance
diagrams.purpose: Students learn to understand the complete exception classification system, recognize the difference between Error and Exception, and identify which exceptions are checked vs unchecked based on their position in the hierarchy

diagrams.title: Try-Catch-Finally Execution Flow
diagrams.description: A flowchart showing the step-by-step execution flow when exception handling blocks are executed, including normal execution and exception scenarios
elements: Start box: Code enters try block
Decision diamond: Does exception occur in try block?
Path A (No exception): Execute try block → Execute finally block → Continue with rest of program
Path B (Exception occurs): Execute try block until exception point → Catch blocks checked in order → Execute matching catch block → Execute finally block → Continue with rest of program
Path C (No matching catch): Execute finally block → Propagate exception up the call stack
Rectangles for code blocks, diamonds for decisions, arrows for flow direction, dashed lines for alternate paths
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the exact sequence of execution in exception handling, when catch blocks are evaluated, and that finally block always executes regardless of whether an exception occurs or not

diagrams.title: Exception Propagation in Call Stack
diagrams.description: A vertical stack diagram showing how an uncaught exception propagates upward through multiple method calls in the call stack
elements: Stack frame 1 (bottom): main() method calling methodA()
Stack frame 2: methodA() calling methodB()
Stack frame 3: methodB() calling methodC()
Stack frame 4 (top): methodC() where exception originates
Arrow showing exception bubble rising from methodC through methodB to methodA
Annotation showing: 'Exception propagates UP the call stack until caught'
Catch block in methodA shown catching the exception
Dotted arrows showing unwinding of stack frames after catch
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the concept of exception propagation, understand that exceptions bubble up through calling methods, and realize why try-catch blocks at appropriate levels can handle exceptions from deeper in the call stack

diagrams.title: Custom Exception Class Structure
diagrams.description: A class diagram showing how to create custom exception classes in Java, showing both checked and unchecked custom exception implementations
elements: Rectangle for Throwable class (parent)
Rectangle for Exception class (inherits from Throwable)
Rectangle for RuntimeException class (inherits from Exception)
Two custom exception classes below: 'MyCheckedException extends Exception' with constructor calling super()
Custom exception class below RuntimeException: 'MyUncheckedException extends RuntimeException' with constructor
Annotations showing: 'Use checked exceptions for recoverable errors' and 'Use unchecked exceptions for programming errors'
Optional: Adding fields like errorCode, timestamp to custom exceptions
diagrams.purpose: Students learn how to create their own exception classes, understand the difference between checked and unchecked custom exceptions, and learn best practices for exception class design

charts.title: Checked vs Unchecked Exceptions
charts.content:
| Feature | Checked Exceptions | Unchecked Exceptions |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Parent Class | java.lang.Exception (not RuntimeException) | java.lang.RuntimeException |
| Compiler Handling | Compiler forces try-catch or throws declaration | Compiler does not enforce handling |
| Detection | At compile time | At runtime |
| Examples | IOException, SQLException, ClassNotFoundException | NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, ArithmeticException |
| Recovery | Expected recoverable conditions | Programming errors, bugs |
| Best Practice | Handle or declare | Fix the code rather than catching |
| When to Use | External resources, validation failures | Logic errors, unexpected conditions |

charts.title: Exception Handling Flow Decision Tree
charts.content:
START → Code executes in try block → EXCEPTION OCCURS? → NO → Execute finally → Continue program → END
YES → Check first catch clause → MATCH? → YES → Execute catch block → Execute finally → Continue program → END
NO → Check next catch clause → MORE CATCHES? → YES → Repeat matching process
NO → Execute finally block → PROPAGATE exception to caller → END
(If finally has exception, it overrides any try/catch exception)

charts.title: Exception Handling Keywords Summary
charts.content:
| Keyword | Purpose | Example Usage |
|---------|---------|---------------|
| try | Defines block to monitor for exceptions | try { // risky code } |
| catch | Handles specific exception type | catch (IOException e) { // handle } |
| finally | Always executes cleanup code | finally { // close resources } |
| throw | Manually throw an exception | throw new MyException("error") |
| throws | Declares exceptions a method may throw | void read() throws IOException { } |
| try-with-resources | Auto-closes resources (Java 7+) | try (FileReader r = new FileReader("f.txt")) { } |

charts.title: throw vs throws Keywords
charts.content:
| Aspect | throw | throws |
|--------|------|-------|
| Purpose | Used to actually throw an exception | Used to declare that a method may throw exceptions |
| Syntax | throw new Exception("message"); | void method() throws IOException { } |
| Location | Inside method body | In method signature |
| What it does | Creates and throws exception instance | Informs compiler/caller about potential exceptions |
| Can throw | Single exception at a time | Multiple exceptions separated by commas |
| Checked Exception | Optional (can throw checked or unchecked) | Mandatory for checked exceptions |
| Example | throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid input"); | public void readFile() throws FileNotFoundException, IOException |

branches: Exception Fundamentals: Event disrupting normal flow, occurs at runtime
Exception Types: Checked (IOException, SQLException) vs Unchecked (RuntimeException)
Keywords: try, catch, finally, throw, throws
Creating Custom Exceptions: Extend Exception (checked) or RuntimeException (unchecked)
Best Practices: Catch specific exceptions, don't swallow exceptions, use finally for cleanup
Common Exceptions: NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, ArithmeticException, ClassNotFoundException, SQLException
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview connecting all exception handling concepts, helping students see relationships between types, keywords, and best practices for exam preparation and practical application
